title: Beyond words – a case study in corpus approaches to interaction and multimodality
author: Schmidt, Thomas
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
While corpus linguistics as a method keeps its focus on quantitative analysis of written language (Mair 2018), recent endeavours have successfully addressed challenges arising in the work with interactive spoken language data (“face-to-face communication”). In our own work, we have shown how basic corpus linguistic methods can be adapted and transferred from written to spoken language to inform analyses combining quantitative and qualitative aspects in a CA paradigm (e.g. Reineke et al. 2023). Other work has combined qualitative and quantitative approaches via systematic manual coding of categories relevant for interactive processes (Luginbühl et al. 2021). More recently, we developed methods for automatically deriving quantitative information from transcribed data specific to the spoken modality (e.g. articulation rates, deviation from standard pronunciation, degree of interactivity or “orality”). This information can also made fruitful in corpus approaches to interaction data (Fandrych et al. 2023).   
  While these methods serve to supplement qualitative analyses with quantitative insights (or vice versa), they remain “text-centered” (Bubenhofer 2022) in so far as they operate mostly on verbal transcripts, largely backgrounding the non-verbal (bodily) dimensions of communication. Integrating the latter type of information into a corpus linguistic paradigm would do justice to the inherently multimodal nature of face-to-face interaction. However, it poses additional challenges in terms of data modelling and analysis methods. It is these challenges which our contribution will present and discuss:   
  Against the background of the work cited above, we look at collections of video recordings of natural interaction whose annotation includes different layers referring to non-verbal modalities (Pitsch 2023) such as manual (free) description or (systematic) coding of gestures, body posture, facial expression, or movement/actions visible in the video image, as well sensor data, such as eye tracking or motion capture. We identify granularity, categorisation and simultaneity as key concepts guiding appropriate improvements and extensions to the existing solutions. Following this methodological reflection, we demonstrate a prototype of a digital environment supporting quantitative and qualitative exploration of the interplay between verbal and non-verbal modalities.   
  Bubenhofer, Noah (2022): Corpus Linguistics in Discourse Analysis: No Bodies and no Practices?   Zeitschrift für Diskursforschung(2), 195-204.   
  Fandrych, C./Meißner, C./Schwendemann, M./Wallner, F. (2023): ZUMAL: Zielgruppenspezifische Gesprächsauswahl aus Korpora gesprochener Sprache. Korpora Deutsch als Fremdsprache. 2023. 3 (1).   S. 13–43.  
  Luginbühl, Martin/ Mundwiler, Vera/ Kreuz, Judith/ Müller-Feldmeth, Daniel/Hauser, Stefan (2021): Quantitative and Qualitative Approaches in Conversation Analysis: Methodological Reflections on a Study of Argumentative Group Discussions.   Gesprächsforschung, 22, 179-236.  
  Mair, Christian (2018): Erfolgsgeschichte Korpuslinguistik? In: Kupietz, Marc/Schmidt, Thomas (Hrsg.): Korpuslinguistik. Berlin/Boston: de Gruyter, 2018.  
  Pitsch, K. (2023): Mensch-Roboter-Interaktion als Forschungsinstrument der Interaktionalen Linguistik. In: Meiler, M., Siefkes, M. (Hrsg.): Linguistische Methodenreflexion im Aufbruch, 119-152.  
  Reineke, Silke/Deppermann, Arnulf/Schmidt, Thomas (2023): Das Forschungs- und Lehrkorpus für Gesprochenes Deutsch (FOLK). Zum Nutzen eines großen annotierten Korpus gesprochener Sprache für interaktionslinguistische Fragestellungen. In: Deppermann, Arnulf/Fandrych, Christian/Kupietz, Marc/Schmidt, Thomas (eds.): Korpora in der germanistischen Sprachwissenschaft. Mündlich, schriftlich, multimedial, 71-102.

  While these methods serve to supplement qualitative analyses with quantitative insights (or vice versa), they remain “text-centered” (Bubenhofer 2022) in so far as they operate mostly on verbal transcripts, largely backgrounding the non-verbal (bodily) dimensions of communication. Integrating the latter type of information into a corpus linguistic paradigm would do justice to the inherently multimodal nature of face-to-face interaction.
